Freelance income is great, but you're right to think ahead — it comes with tax obligations that regular employees don't face, and ignoring them can lead to a surprise bill (plus penalties) when you file.
Looking at your transactions, I can see you received $1,200 from Figma Consulting Client in May. Since this is the situation involving tax implications, I'll explain the concepts educationally. Per CFP Board standards, consult a qualified tax advisor or CPA for advice specific to your situation before making decisions.
The core issue: self-employment tax
As a freelancer, you're responsible for both sides of FICA — the portion your employer normally pays on your behalf. That's 15.3% on net self-employment earnings (12.4% Social Security + 2.9% Medicare), compared to the 7.65% you pay as a W-2 employee at Microsoft.
On top of that, your freelance profit gets added to your ordinary income, which could push you into a higher federal bracket depending on your total annual earnings.
What I'd suggest doing now
1. Set aside money for taxes right away
A solid rule of thumb for freelancers in a moderate income range is to reserve about 25–30% of your freelance income for taxes. On your $1,200 from Figma, that means setting aside roughly $300–$360 into a separate savings account and not touching it.
2. Consider quarterly estimated payments
The IRS expects you to pay taxes as you earn, not just at filing. If your total tax owed from freelance income exceeds $1,000 for the year, you'll generally want to make quarterly estimated payments to avoid an underpayment penalty. The 2026 estimated tax deadlines are typically mid-April, mid-June, mid-September, and mid-January.
3. Track your business expenses
Any legitimate costs tied to your freelance work — software, equipment, a portion of your home office if applicable, professional subscriptions like Adobe (I can see you pay $59.99/month for it) — may be deductible against your freelance income. This reduces the taxable profit and therefore the self-employment tax owed.
4. Keep it separate from your regular income
Your Microsoft payroll is already flowing through your Chase checking account. Keeping freelance deposits clearly identifiable (your Ally savings or a dedicated account) makes record-keeping and tax time much simpler.
One broader note
If freelance income becomes a more regular stream, you might eventually explore setting up a SEP IRA or Solo 401(k), which can let you shelter a meaningful portion of self-employment income from taxes while building retirement savings. That's a longer-term conversation, but worth keeping in mind if the Figma work grows.
